answer:
Based on the provided context chunks, it appears that there are several policies being discussed or suggested to combat AI automation job loss.

One such policy is the suggestion of regulations to guarantee that AI generates new opportunities rather than escalating inequality (L. Littman et al., 2021) (nigar_2025_chunk_183). Additionally, automating monotonous jobs so employees can concentrate on more creative and high-value tasks is also being discussed as a way to mitigate the negative impacts of AI automation (MIT's Work of the Future Initiative, nigar_2025_chunk_183).

Furthermore, assessing job automation risks and resilient alternatives is proposed as a strategy for workers to compete with robots (Paolillo et al., 2022) (demirci_2023_chunk_205). This approach involves understanding how AI impacts various industries to predict the nature of work in the future and develop workforce adaptation plans.

Finally, automating repetitive processes such as data entry, scheduling, and analysis can be a way to reduce the impact of AI automation on certain professions (Goyal and Aneja, 2020) (nigar_2025_chunk_150).

It is worth noting that no specific policies are mentioned in the provided context chunks. However, these suggestions provide some ideas for policymakers and corporate leaders to consider when developing strategies to combat AI automation job loss.

No evidence in corpus for this: "policies" as a general term.
